pages.raw_text:
Probably no public service cor.
poration in Spokane has less regard
for the general public than the
local arm OF the Bell telephone
monopoly, the Pacific States Tele-
phone Co.


The attitude of this corporation
toward its patrons or those seeking
the use of telephones is clear
demonstration of the harmful eff
fects of issuing public utility fran
chises containing no restrictions as
to rates or means Of compulsion as
to giving service. In no other city
probably has this company the alb
solute cinch it has here.


The Pacific States Telephone Co.
in Spokane is operating under a
franchise of questionable title, con-
tanning no provision limiting phone
charges or other restrictions calcu-
lated to give the city check over
its operations.


Contracts made with patrons of
the company seem to be binding
only on the patron and not on the
company. The company is ever
ready to sign up all applicants for
telephone service, but the installa-
ton of the phone is another con-
sideration. The contract provides
that the patron shall be taxed $3.50
in case he desires it cancelled, but
contains no provision compelling
the company to give service in one
month, one year or 10 years. These
contracts simply bind the applicant
for telephone service to the extent
of obligatipg him to use the COM
pany's phone if they choose to give
him one, and complexities matters
somewhat for the prospective
phone user II any other company
should come along that meant bust.
ness.


While this failure to give service
has been experienced in all sections
of the city, 1t Is more particularly
felt in the outlying districts. An
instance that has j,\t come to light
illustrates the way the Spokane
arm of the Bell does business. A
contract was entered into with a
man In Heaths addition for the use
of phone twelve months at $3.50
per month on four party line
After six months had elapsed the
man moved to Lidgerwood and put
in request for change of his
phone. The fact that it had signed
contract TO give this patron tele,
phone service for one year at $1.5O
per month had no weight with the
company, and the patron was asked
to sign a new contract for a year
at an increase of 25 cents per
month for the same class of ser.
vice. The telephone patron objected
for time on the ground that the
company had obligated itself to
give him phone service for a year
at $50 per month, but finding that
his protest would be of no avail he
signed new contract agreeing to
pay tho $175 rate. Time went on
and no phone came. At the end
of the first month he was told IL
would come in the next 30 days
bat no phone was seen.


Things dragged on In this way
the telephone company making
